attributes.stem:
A three-year-old boy is brought into the Emergency Department by his mother with severe breathing difficulties. This morning the child's mother had taken him to the General Practice due to a mild ongoing cough and rhinorrhoea; antibiotics were prescribed, and the mother gave her son the antibiotics an hour ago. He has had multiple croup infections not requiring hospital review, and his vaccination record is up to date.

On examination an inspiratory stridor, increased work of breathing and wheeze are evident. His blood pressure is 70/52mmHg, pulse 162/min, temperature 38.6oC, respiratory rate 46/min and SpO2 94% on room air. The child has an urticarial rash on his anterior chest and neck. His mother reports normal bowel movements and no vomiting.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following management options is most strongly indicated?
attributes.explanation:
This scenario requires identification that the child is having an anaphylactic reaction.

Anaphylaxis has multi-system manifestations and a trigger may not always be easily identified. Anaphylaxis is highly likely in the presence of an acute illness with hypotension or bronchospasm with a highly probably allergy (with or without skin features), or in acute illness with dermatological presentation (hives, pruritis, angioedema, erythema) and at least one other system involvement of respiratory (wheeze, stridor, hypoxaemia), cardiovascular (palpitations, tachycardia/bradycardia, hypotension, pallor, collapse) or gastroenterological (nausea, vomitting, diarrhoea, severe crampy abdominal pain).

While this patient is also showing signs of ongoing infection, this is most likely to be anaphylaxis that occurred after the provision of the antibiotics. Features in the stem that support the diagnosis of anaphylaxis include rapid-onset shortness of breath that first started after antibiotic usage, inspiratory stridor, reduced oxygen saturations and a rash.

IM adrenaline is therefore the correct answer. Adrenaline is the most critical aspect of management and early administration has been consistently shown to improve outcomes. The standard initial dose is 10 mcg/kg - thus the recommended dose for a three year old weighing approximately 15kg would be 0.15mL (150 mcg) of 1:1000 adrenaline. Adrenaline must be administered via the intramuscular route. Repeated doses of IM adrenaline may be required at 5 -15 minutely intervals in up to 20% of patients if there is no improvement due to the short-half life of adrenaline.

IV ceftriaxone is incorrect as it does not treat the most likely cause of the acute onset (anaphylaxis) of respiratory distress. Whilst there is an acute infection, his symptoms were mild before the administration of antibiotics and thus the severity does not indicate any need for IV antibiotics.

Similarly nebulised salbutamol is also incorrect as, whilst this would be effective in an acute asthma attack, based on the overall manifestations of the patient and age of the patient, he is more likely having an anaphylactic response which must be addressed as the first priority.

IV hydrocortisone is incorrect. Despite corticosteroids still being widely used in the management of patients with anaphylaxis, recent evidence (including a 2020 systematic review) has failed to find evidence for the use of corticosteroids in preventing the biphasic reaction. Oral prednisolone or IV hydrocortisone may be used for patients with persistent wheeze however it should not replace adrenaline as an acute treatment.

If 2-3 doses of IM adrenaline are required due to ongoing anaphylaxis or if there is already cardiac arrest occurring, then IV adrenaline infusion should be considered as the next step in management. This should only be initiated under the supervision of paediatric critical care specialists. However, IV noradrenaline would not be used for treatment of anaphylaxis in a child; noradrenaline is mainly used in paediatrics for uncontrolled hypotension in a neonate, or in a baby that has sepsis and is peripherally vasodilated.

Children should be monitored in hospital for at least four hours due to the risk of biphasic reactions. On discharge, parents should be provided appropriate education on anaphylaxis symptoms, EpiPen or Anapen (adrenaline) administration, an anaphylaxis action plan and referral to an immunologist/allergy specialist. Parents should notify the child's school and could consider a MedicAlert bracelet.
learningPoints: IM Adrenaline is first-line management in anaphylaxis
Symptoms of anaphylaxis include angioedema, urticarial rash, inspiratory stridor, hypoxemia, shortness of breath, hives and vomiting.
Anaphylaxis should be considered for syncope or acute upper airway obstruction without rash
references: Chipps BE. Update in pediatric anaphylaxis: a systematic review. Clin Pediatr. 2013 Feb;52(5):451-61. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23393309/

Shaker MS, Wallace DV, Golden DB, Oppenheimer J, Bernstein JA, Campbell RL, et al. Anaphylaxis—a 2020 practice parameter update, systematic review, and Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) analysis. Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology. 2020 Apr;145(4):1082–123. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32001253/

Australian Society of Clinical Immunology and Allergy. Acute Management of Anaphylaxis [Internet]; 2020 [cited 2021 Jun 21]. Available from: https://www.allergy.org.au/images/ASCIA_HP_Guidelines_Acute_Management_Anaphylaxis_2020.pdf
The Royal Children's Hospital of Melbourne. Anaphylaxis. Clinical Practice Guidelines. [online]. Retrieved from: https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/anaphylaxis/
